source.title: Loss of Containment, Fires, and Explosions at Enterprise Products Midstream Gas Plant Pascagoula, Mississippi

event.incident_type: Equipment failure leading to fires and explosions
actions_taken: Operators activated emergency shut down systems within one minute of initial explosion
Emergency systems depressurized the plant and sent process fluids to flare
Multiple local fire departments dispatched approximately four minutes after initial explosion
Decision made to allow fire to burn itself out
Water monitors set up by emergency responders to cool equipment
Site shut down for almost six months
event.summary: On June 27, 2016, at approximately 11:22 p.m., a brazed aluminum heat exchanger (BAHX) on the A-Train process line failed due to thermal fatigue, resulting in a major loss of containment of flammable hydrocarbons. The released hydrocarbons ignited, initiating a series of fires and explosions that caused 13 different ruptures of piping and equipment over approximately 35 minutes. Two workers on the night shift were uninjured. The facility experienced extensive damage and was shut down for almost six months. Enterprise incurred $10.4 million in fire response expenses and a non-cash loss of $7.1 million in damaged assets. A total of 104,000 pounds of emissions were released, with most burned off by fires and flares.
recommendations: 2016-02-I-MS-R1: American Petroleum Institute recommendation on BAHX thermal fatigue management
2016-02-I-MS-R2: GPA Midstream Association recommendation on BAHX integrity management
2016-02-I-MS-R3: GPA Midstream Association recommendation on industry data sharing
2016-02-I-MS-R4: GPA Midstream Association recommendation on BAHX operating guidance
2016-02-I-MS-R5: Jackson County Local Emergency Planning Committee recommendation on community notification and social media use

hazards.name: Pressurized hydrocarbon inventory in cryogenic process
Thermal cycling and temperature fluctuations in brazed aluminum heat exchangers

threats.name: Thermal fatigue crack initiation in BAHX parting sheets and cap sheets
Progression of thermal fatigue cracks to through-wall failures
Rapid temperature changes during production curtailment and compressor shutdowns
Erratic flow conditions causing cyclic thermal stresses
Inadequate inspection and monitoring of BAHX thermal fatigue damage

consequences.name: Cascading equipment failures and secondary ruptures
Financial losses from fire response and asset damage

controls.name: BAHX design and material selection
controls.barrier_role: Inherent design to minimize thermal stress concentration

supporting_text: The probable cause of this incident was the failure of a brazed aluminum heat exchanger (BAHX) due to thermal fatigue.
Thermal fatigue, as a damage mechanism, commonly forms small cracks in BAHXs.

controls.name: Operating temperature rate of change limits per industry guidance
controls.barrier_role: Limit cyclic thermal stresses to prevent fatigue crack initiation
controls.lod_basis: Industry RAGAGEP guidance from ALPEMA, Chart, GPA Midstream, and API

supporting_text: To minimize thermal fatigue in BAHXs, various industry guidance documents recommend limits for maximum cyclic temperature fluctuations during operation and rates of cooling or heating during start-up and shutdown.
Total minutes per year that the inlet and outlet stream temperatures of the ACSR were greater than the industry-recommended ROC guidelines of 1.8°F/min.
PGP's Thermal-Fatiguing Process Conditions included upstream influences and downstream orders to curtail or stop production, compressor shutdowns, and erratic flow.

controls.name: Process data monitoring and temperature sensor installation
controls.barrier_role: Detect abnormal temperature rates of change and thermal cycling
controls.lod_basis: Industry guidance on process data inspection and monitoring

supporting_text: Temperature Sensor Location: Metal temperature sensors installed on the side bars of the ACSR.

ROC data from January 1, 2011, to June 27, 2016, normalized to the A-Train run time for the inlet, outlet, and metal temperatures of the ACSR. ROCs calculated from the metal temperatures are about 10 times less than those for the stream temperatures.
No abnormal alarms or other indicators warned the two PGP personnel of any problems.

controls.name: Mechanical integrity program and BAHX inspection practices
controls.barrier_role: Detect and repair thermal fatigue cracks before catastrophic failure
controls.lod_basis: OSHA PSM mechanical integrity requirements and industry guidance

supporting_text: The absence of a reliable process to ensure the mechanical integrity of the heat exchanger contributed to the catastrophic failure of the equipment.
Leak and Repair History of the ACSR: Historical exchanger repairs at the plant were in the range of $60,000–$150,000.
Recommended Thermal Fatigue Inspection Practices: Industry guidance on methods to inspect exchangers for damage by detecting active leaks during operation.
No records show that workers completed this test to confirm that interpass cracks did not exist between layers 98 and 97.

controls.name: Hazard assessment and process hazard analysis (PHA)
controls.barrier_role: Identify and assess thermal fatigue risk in BAHX operations

supporting_text: Hazard Assessment and Evolving Perception of Thermal Fatigue Risk at PGP: The text discusses how thermal fatigue risk perception evolved over time.
Operators of midstream gas plants need to assess and manage the risk of sudden and catastrophic BAHX rupture scenarios where thermal fatigue plays a contributing role.

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) process for equipment repairs and modifications
controls.barrier_role: Ensure repairs and modifications do not introduce new failure modes
controls.lod_basis: OSHA PSM management of change requirements

supporting_text: MOC form for repairing and blocking passes on the ACSR.
To repair the leaks into layers 2 and 98, workers welded closed layers 99 and 98 as well as layers 1 and 2.
The B-inlet header on the ACSR is marked for cutting during the September 2015 repair. The breach found in the B-inlet header post-incident partially followed the new weld.

controls.name: Emergency shutdown (ESD) system activation
controls.barrier_role: Rapidly depressurize plant and isolate process fluids to limit release magnitude

supporting_text: Within a minute of the initial explosion, the operators activated the emergency shut down systems at the plant and sheltered in the control room.
After ignition, emergency systems depressurized the plant and sent much of the process fluids to a flare.

controls.name: Flare system for safe disposal of hydrocarbons
controls.barrier_role: Safely burn released hydrocarbons to prevent uncontrolled release to atmosphere

supporting_text: Plants like PGP use flares to dispose of flammable gas in the event of an emergency by burning it in a safe and controlled manner.
Most of the released hydrocarbon was burned off by the ensuing fires and flares.

controls.name: Emergency response and fire suppression
controls.barrier_role: Control and extinguish fires to limit damage and consequences
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response procedures and fire department capabilities

supporting_text: The first of several local fire departments was dispatched to the scene approximately four minutes after the initial explosion.

Jackson County Emergency Services, in conjunction with the responding fire departments and PGP management personnel, decided the appropriate course of action would be to allow the fire to burn itself out.
The last fire was extinguished by approximately 6:00 p.m. the next day.

controls.name: Community notification and emergency alert system
controls.barrier_role: Inform community of incident and evacuation needs to minimize off-site consequences
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response procedures and community notification protocols

supporting_text: After the incident, members of a local community organization expressed concern to the CSB that some residents did not know how to respond. They felt uninformed and ill equipped to know if they were in harm's way.

Emergency Response, Community Notification, and the Use of Social Media: The report examines an opportunity to address the need for a more robust and engaged community alert network—one that includes social media.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
